message.content: Adversaries may exfiltrate data using asymmetrically encrypted network protocols distinct from their command and control (C2) channels. This method leverages public-key cryptography, where different keys are used for encryption and decryption on each end of the communication channel. Common protocols include HTTPS, TLS, and SSL, which often switch to symmetric encryption after key exchange. This technique allows adversaries to send data to alternate network locations, complicating detection and interception efforts. Notable examples include APT28 and APT29, which have used HTTPS to exfiltrate data from compromised servers. Tools like Rclone can also facilitate exfiltration over SFTP or HTTPS via WebDAV. Mitigation strategies include monitoring for unusual network traffic patterns, implementing robust encryption key management, and employing data loss prevention (DLP) solutions to detect and block unauthorized data transfers.
